question: What drew you to public policy rather than, say, politics, law, or economics?
lookingFor: A genuine, specific account of what makes policy distinct — shows the applicant understands the field rather than treating it as a vague catch-all.

bullets: Articulate what public policy offers that adjacent fields don't — the translation of ideas into implementable action
Root the answer in a concrete moment, issue, or experience
Show awareness that policy is interdisciplinary by design
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Politics felt too focused on winning arguments and law on resolving disputes after the fact — what I kept coming back to was the moment before, when someone has to decide what government will actually do. I got interested in that through a local planning dispute over a new bus route near my school: technically a small thing, but I watched it involve transport economics, community consultation, and budget trade-offs all at once. Public policy is the field that takes all of that seriously at the same time, and that's why it appealed to me more than any single discipline.

commonMistakes: Giving an answer that could equally describe politics or economics with no real distinction
Vague idealism — 'I want to make the world better' — with nothing specific behind it
Describing the subject as everything they couldn't choose between, implying indecision rather than genuine fit
followUps: Was there a specific policy area you had in mind, or is that still open?
Have you read anything about how policies actually get made — the messiness of it — that still kept you interested?

question: Can you tell us about a real policy or government decision that had a direct impact on your life or community?
lookingFor: Specific, grounded observation — the applicant can connect abstract policy to real effects and think analytically about it rather than just describing it.

bullets: Name a real, specific policy — not a vague gesture at 'austerity' or 'the government'
Explain the mechanism: what did the policy actually do, and how did you see that play out
Move from observation to analysis — what worked, what didn't, what trade-off it revealed
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: When our local council cut library hours under budget pressure, I watched something happen that hadn't been obvious beforehand: it wasn't just inconvenient for readers, it removed the only warm space that several elderly and homeless residents had during the day. The policy intention was financial, but the effect touched public health and social isolation in ways I don't think were fully modelled. That gap between what a policy sets out to do and what it actually does — that's what I want to study. It made me curious about how decision-makers account for second-order effects.

commonMistakes: Choosing a national political controversy without showing any analytical engagement
Staying purely emotional — describing impact without any analysis of why it happened
Picking a policy they clearly read about rather than experienced, and not being able to speak to any detail
followUps: If you'd been advising the council, what would you have explored before making that cut?
Do you think there was a better version of the same decision available?

question: What's the difference between a policy goal and a policy instrument? Can you give an example?
lookingFor: Conceptual clarity that the applicant understands policy is not just intention — they grasp that the choice of instrument shapes outcomes as much as the goal does.

bullets: Define both clearly: a goal is the intended outcome; an instrument is the specific mechanism used to get there
Show that the same goal can be pursued with different instruments, each with different effects
Use a concrete, accurate example
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A policy goal is what you want to achieve — say, reducing sugar consumption. A policy instrument is the specific mechanism you use to get there: that might be a sugar tax, a ban on advertising to children, mandatory traffic-light labelling, or subsidies for healthier alternatives. The important thing is that different instruments create different incentives and distribute costs differently — a tax raises revenue but can be regressive, hitting lower-income families harder. So the instrument choice is itself a political and ethical decision, not just a technical one. I find that layering between intention and implementation one of the most interesting parts of the field.

commonMistakes: Treating the goal and the instrument as the same thing
Using an example but not explaining why it illustrates the distinction
Missing the normative dimension — that instrument choice involves trade-offs, not just efficiency
followUps: Why might a government choose a less effective instrument over a more effective one?
Can you think of a case where the instrument actually undermined the goal?

question: What policy issue are you following most closely right now, and what's your analysis of it?
lookingFor: Up-to-date engagement with a real issue plus genuine analytical thinking — not just an opinion, but evidence of reading and reasoning.

bullets: Name a specific, current issue rather than a broad theme
Show you understand multiple sides or the core trade-off — not just a position
Refer to something you read or researched that shaped your view
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been following the debate over congestion pricing in cities — several places have implemented it, London for the longest, and now New York's scheme has been going back and forth politically. What interests me analytically is that it's a case where the economics are fairly clear — pricing road use reduces congestion and generates revenue for public transport — but the politics are hard because the distributional question hasn't been resolved well: who bears the cost if you can't easily shift to public transport? I've been reading some of the Transport for London evaluations and a paper comparing Stockholm's trial, and the evidence seems to say it works if paired with transit investment, but that pairing is exactly what gets cut when budgets are tight.

commonMistakes: Giving a purely partisan answer that doesn't engage with the trade-offs
Choosing something so broad — 'climate change' or 'inequality' — that no real analysis is possible
Having an opinion but no reading or evidence behind it
followUps: Who do you think bears the burden of that policy, and is that fair?
Has following this issue changed your view on something?

question: Do you think governments follow the evidence when making policy? Should they?
lookingFor: Nuanced thinking about the gap between knowledge and decision-making — the applicant doesn't give a naive yes or a cynical no, but explores why the relationship is genuinely complicated.

bullets: Acknowledge that in practice, governments often don't — and give a reason why, not just a complaint
Distinguish between cases where evidence is contested and cases where it's clear but politically inconvenient
Engage with the normative question: what does 'following the evidence' even mean in a democracy?
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In practice, governments follow evidence selectively — they use it when it supports decisions they're inclined to make, and they're more likely to downplay it when it's inconvenient. Tobacco and sugar are obvious examples where clear evidence was resisted for decades. But I think the question 'should they follow the evidence' is genuinely harder than it looks. Evidence tells you about likely effects; it doesn't tell you how to weigh competing values. A policy that reduces overall obesity rates but hits low-income families harder raises an equity question that evidence alone can't answer. So I'd say: governments should be far more rigorous about using evidence as an input, but pretending it's the whole answer would actually be a distortion of what democratic deliberation is for.

commonMistakes: Saying 'yes, always follow the evidence' without engaging with the values question
Saying 'politicians are corrupt and ignore evidence' with nothing more analytical than that
Treating 'evidence' as one unified thing rather than noting that evidence can be uncertain, contested, or partial
followUps: Can you think of a case where following the evidence would have been the wrong call?
What would 'evidence-based policy' look like in practice if a government took it seriously?

question: Can you walk us through a policy you think failed, and explain why?
lookingFor: Analytical depth — the applicant can diagnose failure in terms of design, implementation, evidence, or political context rather than just saying the outcome was bad.

bullets: Choose a well-evidenced case, not just a personally disliked policy
Distinguish between failure of design, failure of implementation, and failure of political will
Be fair — acknowledge what the policy was trying to achieve before explaining where it went wrong
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The UK's poll tax is a well-documented case I've looked at. The goal — replacing rates with a per-head charge — was actually motivated by a real problem: domestic rates were seen as unfair because they didn't reflect the number of people in a household using services. But the instrument was badly designed because it was flat rather than progressive, which meant a duchess and her cleaner paid the same amount. The implementation also ran ahead of any piloting — Scotland went first as an experiment that wasn't really treated as one. The political and administrative failure is as interesting as the equity failure. It's a good example of what happens when a coherent theory of change isn't tested against distributional reality.

commonMistakes: Choosing a policy that's just politically unpopular without any analytical framework for why it failed
Treating failure as purely intentional wrongdoing rather than design or implementation problems
Not acknowledging what the policy was genuinely trying to solve
followUps: Is there a version of that policy that could have worked?
What process change would have caught the flaw earlier?

question: Public policy research uses both quantitative and qualitative methods. What do each bring, and when does one fall short?
lookingFor: Understanding that methods are tools chosen to match a question — not a preference for one or a dismissal of the other.

bullets: Explain what quantitative methods give you: measurement, causality at scale, pattern detection
Explain what qualitative methods give you: mechanism, context, meaning, what the numbers can't capture
Give a concrete example where relying on only one would have missed something
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Quantitative methods let you measure effects at scale — a randomised control trial or regression can tell you that a policy moved a number, and with enough rigour you can attribute causation. But they often struggle to explain why something worked, or whether it'll work somewhere else with different context. Qualitative work — interviews, ethnography, case studies — gets at the mechanism and the meaning behind the numbers. Take welfare-to-work programmes: administrative data tells you how many people moved into jobs; it often takes qualitative fieldwork to understand why some participants found it demeaning and disengaged. The two methods answer different questions, and honest policy research uses whichever fits, or both.

commonMistakes: Treating quantitative as 'rigorous' and qualitative as 'soft' — or the reverse
Failing to give a concrete example
Not acknowledging that mixed-methods approaches exist
followUps: Have you used either method in any project or research?
Can you think of a policy question that's genuinely hard to answer with any method?

question: When a government wants to change a policy, why is it so hard even when everyone agrees the current situation is bad?
lookingFor: Understanding of path dependency, vested interests, and political economy — that knowledge of 'what's right' doesn't translate automatically into change.

bullets: Introduce the idea that those who benefit from the status quo have concentrated incentives to resist change
Note that costs and benefits of reform are often distributed asymmetrically — losers feel it sharply, winners diffusely
Give a real example, even briefly
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: There's a pattern political scientists call the collective action problem: even when a reform would benefit society overall, the people who'd lose something specific fight much harder than the people who'd each gain a little. Agricultural subsidies are a classic case — farmers are a small, organised group with a lot to lose, while consumers each gain a few pounds a year on their shopping and have little incentive to lobby hard. Add to that the institutional dimension: departments build their procedures around existing rules, and changing the rules creates a period of uncertainty nobody wants to manage. 'Everyone agrees it's bad' is often not quite true either — disagreement about who pays and who gains is usually lurking.

commonMistakes: Blaming inertia entirely on political cowardice with no structural explanation
Missing the distributional point — that it's about who loses and who gains, not just consensus
Giving an answer that would fit any organisation, not specifically government and democratic accountability
followUps: Can you think of a reform that did happen quickly, and what made the difference?
What role can crises play in enabling change that was previously stuck?

question: What is a market failure, and can you give an example where government intervention was clearly justified?
lookingFor: Solid conceptual grasp of why markets sometimes produce inefficient or unfair outcomes — with a clean example and some awareness of when intervention can itself go wrong.

bullets: Define market failure accurately: when the market left to itself produces an outcome that's inefficient or socially undesirable
Give a clear category — externalities, public goods, information asymmetry, or natural monopoly
Choose an example that genuinely illustrates the category and explain the intervention
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A market failure is when the price mechanism doesn't capture all the relevant costs or benefits, so the market equilibrium is inefficient. The classic type is an externality: a factory that pollutes a river imposes costs on downstream communities that don't show up in the price of its product, so it over-produces relative to what's socially optimal. Carbon pricing is a government response to exactly that: by taxing emissions, you try to bring the external cost inside the price signal. I find the information asymmetry examples interesting too — health insurance markets fail partly because patients and insurers know different things, which is one reason purely private health systems tend to leave people uncovered. That's a good justification for some form of public provision or regulation.

commonMistakes: Conflating market failure with markets being 'bad' in general
Giving an example that's actually a distributional concern rather than a market failure
Not acknowledging that government intervention can also fail
followUps: Can you think of a case where government intervention made things worse?
How would you decide whether to regulate, tax, or ban in response to an externality?

question: What is a 'nudge' in policy terms, and do you think it's an ethically acceptable way for governments to influence behaviour?
lookingFor: Conceptual accuracy on nudge theory plus genuine engagement with the paternalism objection — not just enthusiasm for behavioural policy.

bullets: Define a nudge accurately: changing choice architecture to guide behaviour without restricting options or changing financial incentives
Give a clear example — organ donation opt-out, pension auto-enrolment, healthy food placement
Engage honestly with the ethical objection: is it manipulation, and does it matter that people aren't told?
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A nudge is a change to the way choices are presented that predictably shifts behaviour without removing options — auto-enrolling people into pension schemes is the canonical example. It works because our choices are sensitive to defaults in ways we don't always notice. The ethical question is real: if the government is using psychology to steer you without fully telling you, is that a form of manipulation, even if it's for your benefit? I think my view is that it's acceptable when the nudge is transparent, when it aligns with the stated preferences of most people — most people say they want to save more — and when opting out is genuinely easy. What I'd be more uncomfortable with is nudges used to serve state interests rather than individual wellbeing, or where the paternalism is covering for something the government doesn't want to debate openly.

commonMistakes: Confusing a nudge with a mandate or a tax — the distinction is that nudges preserve free choice
Purely enthusiastic about behavioural policy without engaging with the paternalism concern
Not having a concrete example ready
followUps: Where would you draw the line between an acceptable nudge and unacceptable manipulation?
Can you think of a nudge that backfired or had unintended effects?

question: Is economic inequality a policy problem? What would you need to know before deciding how to address it?
lookingFor: The applicant distinguishes between inequality as a fact and inequality as a problem — and knows that diagnosis must precede prescription.

bullets: Note that whether inequality is a problem depends partly on its causes and partly on the values you bring to it
Distinguish between inequality of outcomes and inequality of opportunity
Identify the key empirical questions a policymaker needs to answer before choosing an instrument
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Whether it's a problem depends on which form of inequality you're looking at and what's driving it. Most people across the political spectrum are more concerned about inequality of opportunity — where you start determines where you end — than about inequality of outcomes per se. The empirical questions matter a lot before designing any response: Is inequality rising or stable? Is it being driven by returns to education, geography, inherited wealth, wage stagnation at the bottom, or something else? A policy aimed at the wrong cause is likely to be ineffective. I'd also want to understand mobility — high inequality with high mobility looks very different from high inequality that's entrenching across generations. The diagnosis shapes whether you're looking at tax and transfer, early childhood investment, housing reform, or labour market policy.

commonMistakes: Jumping straight to a policy prescription before diagnosing what's actually happening
Treating inequality as an obviously good or obviously bad thing without engaging with the empirical and normative complexity
Conflating inequality and poverty — they're related but distinct
followUps: Is there a level of inequality you'd consider acceptable, and why?
Which intervention do you think has the best evidence behind it?

subtopic: Public goods and the role of the state
question: What makes something a 'public good' in the technical sense, and why does that matter for policy?
lookingFor: Accurate understanding of non-rivalry and non-excludability — and the ability to draw the policy implication without prompting.

bullets: Define the two properties correctly: non-rivalrous (one person's use doesn't reduce availability for others) and non-excludable (you can't easily stop non-payers from benefiting)
Explain why private markets underprovide public goods — the free-rider problem
Give a clear example and connect it to why state provision or funding is justified
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A public good in the economic sense has two properties: it's non-rivalrous — my listening to a radio broadcast doesn't stop you — and non-excludable — once the signal is broadcast, you can't easily stop non-payers from receiving it. Because of those two features, private markets underprovide them: if you can't charge for something, you won't produce it at the level society actually wants. National defence is the textbook case — you can't defend some citizens but not others in the same city, so the market would never fund it adequately. The policy implication is that some form of collective provision, through taxation and public spending, is needed. What I find interesting is how technology changes the boundary — streaming makes content excludable in ways it wasn't before, which shifts the policy question.

commonMistakes: Confusing 'public good' with 'good for the public' — many things are good for the public without being public goods technically
Getting non-rivalry and non-excludability the wrong way round
Not linking the definition to the free-rider problem and the market failure
followUps: Is the internet a public good? What about clean air?
Can a public good be privately provided? How?

question: If you were asked to evaluate whether a job training programme actually reduced unemployment, how would you approach it?
lookingFor: Awareness of the counterfactual problem — you can't observe what would have happened without the programme — and knowledge of at least one method for addressing it.

bullets: Name the core challenge: the counterfactual — we need to know what would have happened to participants without the programme
Describe at least one credible method: randomised control trial, difference-in-differences, or regression discontinuity
Acknowledge practical and ethical constraints — you can't always randomise, and who gets left out matters
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The core problem is the counterfactual: comparing participants before and after gives a misleading picture if they'd have found jobs anyway during a strong economy. The cleanest solution is a randomised control trial — you assign applicants randomly to the programme or a control group, and the difference in employment outcomes between them is attributable to the programme. In practice that's often ethically or politically difficult; if the programme is good, you're denying it to the control group. So you might use a quasi-experimental approach instead — if the programme has an eligibility cutoff, a regression discontinuity design compares people just above and below it, who are otherwise very similar. I'd also want to think about what employment means: short-term job placement or sustained earnings over years, and whether the types of jobs people got were good ones.

commonMistakes: Saying 'compare unemployment before and after' without recognising the counterfactual problem
Assuming a randomised trial is always feasible or ethical
Ignoring outcome definition — what counts as success
followUps: What would you do if a randomised trial was ruled out for ethical reasons?
How would you handle the risk that the programme creams off the most employable applicants?

question: How does globalisation limit what national governments can do in terms of domestic policy?
lookingFor: Awareness that policy is made in a global context that constrains national choices — trade rules, capital mobility, international agreements — without overstating the constraint.

bullets: Give at least two concrete mechanisms: capital mobility limiting tax rates, trade agreements constraining regulation, international norms shaping what's politically feasible
Avoid the 'globalisation makes government irrelevant' overstatement — show the constraint is real but bounded
Connect it to a real policy example
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: There are a few real mechanisms. Capital mobility is the most direct — if corporate tax rates diverge too far from competitors, firms and profits shift elsewhere, which constrains how far governments can raise those rates. Trade agreements limit the ability to use certain regulatory or subsidy tools: WTO rules restrict how you favour domestic producers. And there's a softer convergence effect — international norms and peer pressure push policy in similar directions, especially on things like central bank independence or deficit limits. But I think the 'states are helpless' argument goes too far: most welfare spending, education, housing, and healthcare is domestically financed and domestically administered, and those areas vary enormously between countries with similar exposure to global markets. The constraint is real in some domains and relatively weak in others.

commonMistakes: Claiming globalisation makes national policy irrelevant — an overstated position
Only naming one mechanism (usually tax competition) and not going deeper
Forgetting that international institutions also enable some policies that wouldn't happen otherwise — global health coordination, climate agreements
followUps: Can you think of an area where globalisation actually enabled a domestic policy that wouldn't have happened otherwise?
How should governments respond to this constraint — pull back from global integration or accept it?

question: What are the arguments for devolving policy decisions to regional or local governments rather than making them centrally?
lookingFor: A balanced account of the subsidiarity argument plus its limits — the applicant sees that local is not always better.

bullets: State the core subsidiarity argument: local governments have better information about local needs and preferences
Add the accountability and experimentation arguments
Note the genuine limits: equity across regions, scale economies, race to the bottom
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The strongest argument is information: a local council usually understands local needs, geography, and preferences better than a central ministry. There's also an accountability argument — local politicians are more visible to residents and more directly answerable. And federal or devolved systems allow policy experimentation: different regions can try different approaches and you can observe what works. But the limits are real. If rich regions can raise more revenue, devolution can deepen inequality between places — you get a postcode lottery. Some services have economies of scale that require national coordination. And there's the 'race to the bottom' risk on things like environmental standards or labour regulation, where competition between regions drives standards down. So the answer to how much devolution is appropriate depends quite heavily on which policy area you're talking about.

commonMistakes: Treating local government as always better without any of the countervailing arguments
Not knowing what subsidiarity means if the word comes up
Missing the equity point — that devolution can entrench geographic inequality
followUps: Which areas of policy do you think should never be devolved, and why?
Is there a difference between political devolution and administrative decentralisation?

question: There are several policy instruments to reduce carbon emissions — taxes, cap-and-trade, regulations, subsidies. How would you think about choosing between them?
lookingFor: The applicant can apply an evaluative framework to instrument choice — not just know the instruments exist, but reason about how to choose.

bullets: Briefly describe each main instrument type
Apply a framework: efficiency, distributional impact, certainty of outcome, political feasibility
Acknowledge that in practice, combinations are used and context matters
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Carbon taxes and cap-and-trade are both price-based and broadly efficient — they let emitters find the cheapest abatement — but they differ in certainty: a tax gives price certainty but uncertain quantity, while cap-and-trade caps the quantity but lets the price float. Regulations — fuel efficiency standards, building codes — are less flexible but politically easier to sell because they're less visible than a price increase. Subsidies for renewables reduce emissions but don't directly penalise fossil fuels, so they work better as a complement. I'd choose based on the problem: if I need a hard limit on total emissions, cap-and-trade or a regulation suits; if I want to encourage innovation broadly, subsidies work; if I want revenue to recycle into households to offset regressivity, a carbon tax makes sense. In practice most serious climate policy is a mix because no single instrument covers all the gaps.

commonMistakes: Only knowing one instrument — usually 'a carbon tax' — and not the landscape
Not engaging with distributional or political feasibility dimensions
Treating this as a purely technical question without acknowledging the political economy
followUps: How would you design a carbon tax to address its regressivity?
Why have some countries that introduced carbon taxes later reversed or weakened them?

question: A public health intervention reduces deaths but requires restricting individual freedoms. How do you think about that trade-off?
lookingFor: Structured ethical reasoning — the applicant can apply a framework (utilitarian, rights-based, proportionality) and doesn't just pick a side without reasoning.

bullets: Name the tension clearly: aggregate welfare vs. individual rights
Apply at least two frameworks and show what each gives you
Identify the key variables that determine where to draw the line in any specific case
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: There's a genuine tension between consequentialist thinking — a restriction that saves many lives produces a lot of aggregate good — and a rights-based view that says individuals have liberties the state can't override just because the numbers favour it. I don't think either is wholly right. In practice I'd ask: how serious and certain is the harm we're preventing — and not just to the individual but to others? Is the restriction proportionate and temporary? Are there less restrictive means that would work nearly as well? And has there been democratic deliberation? Mask mandates during a pandemic feel different from mandatory vaccination because the magnitude and reversibility differ, and the spillover onto others is different. I lean toward a proportionality framework: the threshold for justifying a restriction should scale with how serious the right being overridden is, and how certain and substantial the benefit.

commonMistakes: Picking one side definitively without reasoning through the other
Treating it as a simple trolley problem when it involves institutional and democratic dimensions
Not naming any ethical framework — just an instinct
followUps: Does your framework change if the group most affected by the restriction is already marginalised?
Who should have the final say — elected politicians, public health officials, or courts?

question: What new policy challenges has the rise of digital platforms and AI created that governments weren't prepared for?
lookingFor: An engaged, current understanding of at least two specific challenges — not a general worry about 'technology', but substantive issues a policymaker would recognise.

bullets: Identify specific, substantive challenges: misinformation, algorithmic accountability, data privacy, platform market power, or AI in high-stakes decisions
Explain why existing frameworks struggle — what made the old tools inadequate
Show some awareness of how governments have tried to respond and where those responses fall short
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A few that I think are genuinely hard. First, algorithmic accountability: when a platform's recommendation algorithm amplifies content that keeps users engaged — including harmful content — who is responsible and how do you regulate something you can't fully audit without access to the code? Traditional product liability or broadcasting regulation doesn't map neatly. Second, AI in public decision-making: benefits offices in several countries have used automated systems to flag fraud or assess eligibility, and there have been cases of systematic error disproportionately affecting already-marginalised groups — the Dutch childcare benefits scandal is a well-documented example. Third, data — the sheer volume of data platforms hold creates power asymmetries that competition law wasn't designed to address. Governments have been playing catch-up, and the EU's AI Act and Digital Markets Act are interesting experiments, but they're still early.

commonMistakes: Vague answers about 'social media being bad' without substantive policy content
Not knowing any specific cases or legislative attempts to respond
Treating technology policy as purely a technical problem rather than a governance one
followUps: Do you think the EU or the US is taking the better regulatory approach to AI, and why?
Should governments use AI in their own decision-making, and with what safeguards?

question: Housing is unaffordable in many cities. Why has policy struggled to solve this, and what would you consider doing?
lookingFor: The applicant understands the political economy of planning and housing — why it's a hard problem despite being a technically tractable one — and can discuss instruments with some realism.

bullets: Diagnose the root cause accurately: restricted housing supply relative to demand, often protected by planning systems
Explain the political economy obstacle: existing homeowners who vote benefit from high prices and oppose new supply
Propose at least one instrument and be honest about its limits
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The basic economics are clear: in many cities demand for housing has grown faster than supply for decades, and planning restrictions have constrained supply. The puzzle is why governments haven't fixed something where the diagnosis is fairly well-agreed. The political economy explanation is strong: homeowners form a large, voting bloc with a financial interest in high prices, and renters are more dispersed and less organised. New developments face concentrated opposition from neighbours, and local politicians respond to that. So you get a structural under-supply. What I'd consider: reforming planning permissions to allow higher density near transport hubs, reducing the scope for local veto on individual developments, and social housing investment to provide supply directly. None of these are painless — denser cities require infrastructure investment, and reducing homeowner political power means taking on a large interest group. But I think the evidence that supply matters is strong enough that a policymaker has to take it seriously.

commonMistakes: Blaming developers and land-banking without engaging with the planning and political economy argument
Proposing rent controls as the main solution without acknowledging the supply effects
Not knowing the political reason this is hard even when the economics are understood
followUps: Do rent controls help or hurt housing affordability in the long run?
How would you build a political coalition for planning reform if homeowners oppose it?

question: Should technocrats — experts with no electoral mandate — have more power over policy, or should policy always be democratically accountable?
lookingFor: Genuine engagement with the tension between expertise and democratic legitimacy — the applicant doesn't dismiss either side.

bullets: Acknowledge the real argument for technocracy: short-termism, voter ignorance of technical complexity, protection from populism
Acknowledge the democratic objection: legitimacy, values, accountability when things go wrong
Land on a principled position about where to draw the line — which questions are technical and which are value-laden
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The argument for technocracy is real: some questions have right answers that require expertise — central bank interest rate decisions, vaccine safety — and democratic processes can be distorted by short-termism or misinformation. Central bank independence is probably the strongest example of delegated technocracy that works reasonably well. But I don't think you can extend that very far. Most policy questions aren't purely technical — they involve trade-offs between values that citizens are entitled to deliberate about. The Eurozone debt crisis showed what happens when technocratic decisions — austerity conditions — impose real suffering on populations who had no say. My view is that the line should be: technocrats have authority over technical implementation within parameters set democratically, but the parameters themselves — who bears the burden, which goals we're pursuing — need democratic accountability. The mechanism for error matters too: if an expert body makes a bad call, there needs to be a route to accountability.

commonMistakes: Fully endorsing technocracy as 'obviously right because experts know better'
Fully rejecting expertise and treating all decisions as equally democratic
Not giving any concrete example — this needs to be grounded to be convincing
followUps: Can you think of a case where democratic override of expert advice led to a better outcome?
How should an expert body be held accountable when it makes a costly mistake?

subtopic: A book or research that shaped thinking
question: Is there a book, article, or piece of research that's shaped how you think about public policy?
lookingFor: Genuine intellectual engagement — the applicant can explain what they read and what it changed or confirmed in their thinking, not just name-drop.

bullets: Name something specific — a title, an author, a paper
Explain what argument or finding it made
Connect it to how it changed or sharpened your thinking
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein's Nudge had a real effect on how I think about the relationship between individual choice and state influence. Their argument is that choice architecture is inescapable — if you're designing a cafeteria, you have to put the food somewhere — so the question isn't whether to influence behaviour but how. I found it compelling but also started noticing the objections more clearly as I read further: if nudges work by exploiting cognitive biases rather than engaging people's reasons, there's something condescending about it even when the outcomes are good. That tension — between effective and respectful — is something I keep coming back to when I think about policy design.

commonMistakes: Naming a book without being able to say what it actually argues
Choosing something too obvious and not being able to show what specific idea stayed with them
Pretending to have read something they haven't — panels notice quickly
followUps: What was the most persuasive objection to the argument you found?
Has anything you've read since challenged that view?

question: Can you give an example of a country whose policy approach in a particular area you find interesting — and explain why it works or doesn't?
lookingFor: An ability to reason comparatively — the applicant understands that policy context travels, but institutions and politics don't always translate.

bullets: Name a specific country and a specific policy area — not a vague 'Scandinavia is good at things'
Explain the mechanism: why does it work there?
Engage honestly with whether it would transfer — and what the conditions of transfer are
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Finland's approach to homelessness is one I've read about quite a bit — the Housing First model, which reversed the traditional logic of requiring people to address addiction or mental health before giving them stable housing. The evidence suggests stable housing is a precondition for addressing those other things, not the reward for having addressed them. Finland has significantly reduced long-term homelessness over twenty years. What I find analytically interesting is the transfer question: it works in part because Finland has a relatively centralised housing system and strong municipal capacity. In the UK or the US, where housing is more fragmented and local governments have fewer resources, the model is harder to implement at scale. That doesn't mean it's not worth trying, but it means you can't just copy the headline without understanding the institutional conditions.

commonMistakes: Vague references to 'Scandinavian countries' without specifics
Not engaging with why it might not transfer
Choosing a case they clearly haven't researched beyond a headline
followUps: What would need to change institutionally in the UK for that approach to work?
Is there a counter-example — a policy that looked good abroad but failed on transfer?

question: What are the main arguments for progressive versus flat taxation, and which do you find more persuasive?
lookingFor: Accurate command of both positions plus a reasoned personal view — not hedging but also not ignoring the genuine arguments on each side.

bullets: State the progressive case: declining marginal utility of income; those with more can contribute more with less relative sacrifice
State the flat-tax case: simplicity, equal treatment in percentage terms, reduced disincentive to earn
Give a reasoned personal position that engages with the strongest counter-argument
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The case for progressive taxation rests largely on the idea of diminishing marginal utility: an extra hundred pounds matters far more to someone on a low income than to someone on a very high one, so taking a smaller percentage from the lower-income earner imposes more real sacrifice. A progressive system also reflects a view that the state should reduce the concentration of wealth over time. The flat-tax argument is mainly about simplicity and neutrality: everyone pays the same rate, which is facially equal, and there's less distortion to work incentives at the top. I find the progressive case more persuasive — not because simplicity doesn't matter, but because the diminishing marginal utility argument is empirically supported and the incidence of a flat tax is regressive in practice when you account for how people at different income levels spend and save. I'd be open to simplification of the progressive structure, but the flat rate itself seems hard to justify on the welfare arguments.

commonMistakes: Refusing to give a view — hedging on every normative question is not intellectual rigour, it's evasion
Not knowing the diminishing marginal utility argument — the core economic case for progressivity
Conflating tax rate and tax revenue — a flat rate doesn't mean everyone pays the same amount
followUps: At what point does progressive taxation start creating significant work disincentives?
Is wealth taxation more or less justifiable than income taxation, in your view?

question: What is the difference between healthcare policy and public health policy, and why does the distinction matter?
lookingFor: The applicant understands that healthcare treats individuals while public health acts on populations and upstream determinants — and can say why this matters for resource allocation.

bullets: Define the distinction clearly: healthcare is treatment of individual patients; public health is prevention, population health, and determinants
Give examples that illustrate what public health policy looks like in practice
Explain the resource allocation implication — public health is chronically under-resourced relative to its returns
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Healthcare policy is primarily about how we organise and fund treatment for people who are already sick — hospitals, GPs, prescription coverage. Public health policy operates further upstream: vaccination programmes, smoking bans, water quality standards, school meals — interventions aimed at preventing illness before it happens, often acting on whole populations rather than individuals. The distinction matters enormously for resource allocation. A pound spent on childhood nutrition or smoking cessation generally generates a much larger return in prevented illness than a pound spent on treating the downstream consequences, but the benefits of prevention are diffuse, long-run, and invisible — you don't see the people who didn't get ill. Treatment is immediate and politically salient. That dynamic explains why, across most healthcare systems, public health is chronically underfunded relative to its actual impact on population health.

commonMistakes: Treating public health and healthcare as synonyms
Not being able to give concrete examples of public health policy — listing hospitals instead
Missing the political economy of why prevention is underfunded
followUps: How would you make the political case for shifting more spending to prevention?
Is mental health a public health issue, a healthcare issue, or both?

question: Why do you want to study Public Policy at this university specifically, rather than another course or institution?
lookingFor: Genuine, specific motivation tied to this programme — the applicant has researched it and can articulate what makes this course the right fit for where they want to go.

bullets: Name something specific to the programme — a module, a faculty specialism, a placement opportunity, a research centre — not just the ranking
Connect it to a concrete academic interest or career direction
Show you understand what makes the programme distinctive, implying you looked at alternatives
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I looked at several programmes and what drew me here specifically is the combination of your quantitative policy methods module and the applied project in the second year — I want rigour in how I evaluate evidence, but I also want to practise translating it into actual recommendations, and most programmes offer one or the other. I was also interested in the work your urban governance research centre is doing on devolution and regional inequality, which connects to the area I most want to focus on. I've read a couple of the papers coming out of that group and found the framing on fiscal decentralisation directly relevant to questions I'd been thinking about from following debates on local government funding. It felt like a place where my interests would be taken seriously as research questions, not just topics.

commonMistakes: Generic praise that could describe any university — 'excellent reputation, great city'
Mentioning the ranking but nothing substantive about the course
Not knowing what actually distinguishes the programme — reveals the applicant hasn't done their research
followUps: Which module are you most looking forward to, and what do you hope to get out of it?
What would you contribute to the cohort's discussions?
